question: Why were only 50 HumanEval items used instead of the full 164, and does this subset selection introduce cherry-picking bias that inflates the 32%→40% improvement?
The JSON tool-call evaluation uses a custom metric—what is the exact rubric, and have you validated scoring fidelity against human judgment on at least 100 samples?
With only 50 training steps, how do you rule out that the 0%→92% tool-call improvement reflects overfitting to a narrow prompt distribution rather than genuine generalization?
The SFT+GRPO complementarity relies on a specific ordering—was SFT-then-GRPO compared against GRPO-then-SFT and GRPO-alone at matched compute budgets?
Across 17 cloud GPU runs, what was the total FLOPs budget, and how were hyperparameters selected—grid search, random search, or manual tuning?
Training reward at 30.5% is reported as single number—was it measured across seeds, and if not, how confident are you the 30.5% vs 83.3% discrepancy is not seed-specific?
The 3B-4B capacity threshold is drawn from few model sizes with no parametric test—have you fit a sigmoid or threshold regression with CIs on the breakpoint?
MoE routing volatility at 2.43x—what is the denominator metric (entropy, load imbalance?) and is this difference statistically significant under bootstrap or permutation test?
Code improvement from 32% to 40% on 50 items = 4 problems. Have you reported Fisher's exact test p-value? Is this statistically significant at p<0.05?
Cross-seed stability with 5 seeds and SD=2.2%—have you compared this variance to published GRPO baselines on the same benchmark to show your stability is meaningfully lower?
ToolBench, APIBench, and ToolAlpaca all study tool-calling fine-tuning with rigorous held-out evaluations—why are these not cited, and how does your 0%→92% compare?
DeepSeekMath and DAPO both extend GRPO with clip-higher and overlong penalties for math—position your GSM8K findings against these GRPO variants, not just vanilla PPO.
The SFT+GRPO complementarity echoes ReST, STaR, and iterative DPO—how does the paper differentiate from established warm-starting RL from supervised demonstrations?
Concurrent work STILL and Tiny-GRPO target sub-3B models—have these been reviewed? If they report similar capacity thresholds, does that strengthen or weaken your novelty?
MoE routing volatility should be contextualized against MoE training instability literature (Switch Transformer, GLaM, Mixtral)—without this, reviewers cannot assess if 2.43x is surprising.
The term 'agentic LLM fine-tuning' is in the title but never defined—provide an operational definition distinguishing agentic from non-agentic fine-tuning in GRPO context.
The 'novel' vs 'confirmatory' classification criteria are implicit—add a Methods subsection stating what evidence threshold was used for this classification.
Audit every figure caption to be self-contained per NeurIPS standards—a reader should interpret the result without reading main text, especially the two-phase learning curves.
The paper conflates 'synthetic-real gap' (data distribution) with 'train-test reward discrepancy' (metric alignment)—these are distinct failure modes; name and analyze them separately.
Group sizes 4-16 and LoRA ranks 8-64 are mentioned in passing—add a configuration table mapping each result to its exact hyperparameter settings to prevent ambiguity.
The 3B-4B threshold is interpolated from only two model sizes (Qwen2.5-3B and Qwen3.5-4B)—reframe as hypothesis requiring validation rather than established finding.
With confirmatory findings labeled as such, contribution reduces to 3 novel claims—abstract and intro should foreground these three specifically rather than listing all equally.
MoE routing volatility is purely observational—does the paper propose any mechanism (e.g., reward gradient interfering with load balancing loss) to elevate from measurement to contribution?
Cross-seed stability under multi-seed eval is standard reporting practice, not a finding—reframe as rigor unless you show GRPO is more stable than alternative RL algorithms.
Consider whether a unified mechanistic narrative (GRPO succeeds when reward is dense and verifiable) would strengthen framing versus treating each task as separate report.
All models are Qwen/Llama—do capacity threshold and MoE volatility generalize to Mistral, Phi-3, or Gemma, or are they artifacts of Qwen-specific design?
At what step count do improvements saturate, and would extending to 200-500 steps change the capacity threshold finding or close the training/test reward gap?
Would the 92% tool-call accuracy transfer to different schemas (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google function-calling formats), or is the model learning schema-specific patterns?
The paper studies mostly single-turn tool calling—how does the capacity threshold change for multi-turn agentic trajectories with chained tool calls?
GSM8K may be partially saturated for 8B models—do GRPO training dynamics hold on harder benchmarks like MATH-500 or AIME where the task is genuinely unsolved?
No ablation on group size (4 vs 8 vs 16)—since group size controls GRPO advantage estimator variance, this is the most important hyperparameter to ablate.
LoRA rank-speed tradeoff is reported but accuracy vs rank curve is missing—add ablation at ranks 8, 16, 32, 64 to determine if tradeoff is monotonic or has a knee.
SFT+GRPO complementarity requires matched-compute control where GRPO alone runs 2x steps—without this, SFT may just provide better initialization benefiting any RL algorithm.
To isolate GRPO from reward design, include SFT baseline trained on GRPO-generated rollouts that received positive reward—separates policy optimization from data curriculum.
Tool calling reward function is not ablated—is the 0%→92% gain driven by reward signal structure (format, correctness, partial credit) rather than GRPO algorithm?
Training reward at 30.5% while test 83.3% suggests reward misspecification—provide formal analysis of why optimizing training reward doesn't maximize test accuracy and implications for reward design.
Two-phase learning mirrors curriculum learning theory and easy-to-hard generalization—grounding in theory would predict when phase transition occurs as function of capacity and reward sparsity.
GRPO advantage normalizes within group—provide theoretical argument for why group sizes 4-16 are appropriate for observed reward scales, or cite original GRPO paper's guidance.
Capacity threshold implies phase transition in model expressivity—is there theoretical basis (scaling laws, NTK, circuit interpretability) for why 3B-4B enables qualitatively different RL behavior?
MoE volatility could stem from load balancing loss conflicting with policy gradient—formalize as optimization interference and propose mitigation even if not experimentally validated.
Absence of PPO/REINFORCE baselines is a rejection risk—prepare rebuttal why GRPO is appropriate primary comparison, and add at least REINFORCE baseline on tool-calling if time permits.
50 training steps may be questioned as meaningful RL—prepare compute-normalized comparison showing 50 GRPO steps achieves parity with or outperforms more SFT steps on held-out eval.
Custom tool-calling eval lacks standardization—either adopt BFCL/ToolBench-eval for subset of results, or release evaluation code and dataset for reproducibility verification.
Qwen3 models have existing post-training RLHF—reviewers may argue GRPO is unwinding alignment rather than clean RL learning. Address by reporting results on base (non-instruct) checkpoint.
17-run count may be flagged as insufficient for breadth of claims—prepare table mapping each claim to specific supporting runs so reviewers can assess well-supported vs exploratory claims.
Provide a practitioner decision flowchart (model size, task type, data) mapping to recommended GRPO configuration, making empirical findings directly actionable.
Specify exact repository structure, license, and data that cannot be released due to cloud GPU provider licensing constraints for full reproducibility.
LoRA rank-speed tradeoff has cost implications—quantify wall-clock time and GPU-hour cost at each rank so practitioners can decide without rerunning ablations.
Cloud GPU variability (hardware, network I/O, preemption) introduces noise—report hardware config, framework versions (PyTorch, HF Transformers, TRL), and run-to-run variance.
Sub-3B GRPO failure has cost-saving implications—quantify as negative result with confidence (models below 3B show less than X% improvement) so it can be cited as engineering guideline.
